title: Severe pain out of proportion to exam

chart.oneLiner: 72F with atrial fibrillation presents with sudden, severe, diffuse abdominal pain that seems out of proportion to a relatively benign abdominal exam.

opening: The pain came on all of a sudden and it's unbearable — but the doctor barely pressing on my belly doesn't seem to match how bad it feels.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 72-year-old woman, in severe distress from pain, restless. Cooperative.

HPI: SUDDEN onset of severe, diffuse, constant abdominal pain ~3h ago, far worse than the exam findings suggest. Associated nausea, vomiting, and one loose (later bloody) stool. Known atrial fibrillation; admits she stopped her blood thinner a couple weeks ago (only if asked about medications/anticoagulation).
PMH: atrial fibrillation, hypertension, prior TIA. MEDS: was on apixaban — self-discontinued; metoprolol; amlodipine. SOCIAL: former smoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: distressed, pale, diaphoretic.
- Vitals: BP 108/66, HR 118 IRREGULARLY irregular, RR 22, 37.6°C, SpO2 96% RA.
- Abdomen: soft, mild diffuse tenderness EARLY without rebound or rigidity (pain out of proportion to exam); bowel sounds reduced. (Peritoneal signs would develop late once infarction occurs.)
- Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmur. Rectal: occult/frank blood if examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the stopped anticoagulation unless asked about medications, blood thinners, or adherence.
- The irregular pulse and the pain-out-of-proportion discrepancy are key — reveal the rhythm if the pulse/heart is examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Lactate: Elevated (e.g., 4.5) — a critical clue to bowel ischemia.
labs.CBC: WBC 18.0 with left shift, Hgb 13.5, Plt 250.
labs.BMP: HCO3 low with metabolic acidosis; Cr 1.3.
labs.CTA_abdomen: CT angiography of the abdomen: filling defect/occlusion of the superior mesenteric artery; segmental bowel wall changes; no free air yet.
labs.StoolEvaluation: Blood in the stool is documented (consistent with mucosal ischemia). Provided as a clinical/diagnostic finding; no sensitive exam is required of the student.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no discrete P waves) — the likely embolic source. No acute ST elevation.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'Atrial Fibrillation' library page — download an example tracing that matches: Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no discrete P waves) — the likely embolic sour … and self-host with credit to litfl.com.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray (upright)
cxr.findings: Nonspecific; importantly NO free air early (free air would indicate perforation from infarcted bowel).
cxr.verify: Pick a CC normal upright chest film (no subdiaphragmatic free air).
cxr.imageDescription: nonspecific upright chest radiograph with no free air under the diaphragm
searchTerms: normal upright chest x-ray no free air
upright chest radiograph no subdiaphragmatic free air
cxr.clinicalVignette: An 80-year-old man with severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam has an upright chest film.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Acute mesenteric ischemia (leading & CANT-MISS,9; accepts mesenteric ischemia/SMA embolus). Perforated viscus (CANT-MISS,4). Ruptured AAA (CANT-MISS,3). Bowel obstruction (core,2). Pancreatitis (core,2). Names mesenteric ischemia with the key clue — pain out of proportion + AF + embolic risk (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute mesenteric ischemia. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Perforated viscus; Ruptured AAA. Also reasonable: Bowel obstruction; Pancreatitis.

coreActions.item: Names mesenteric ischemia with the key clue — pain out of proportion + AF + embolic risk

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Serum lactate (CANT-MISS,4). CT angiography of the abdomen (CANT-MISS,5). EKG (3). CBC (2). BMP / blood gas for acidosis (1). PENALTY: relying on plain films alone or delaying CTA (-3); shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; Lactate; CBC; BMP; LFTs; CTA abdomen; Stool studies.

penalties.item: relying on plain films alone or delaying CTA

contraindicatedTests: relying on plain films alone or delaying CTA

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no P waves); no acute ST elevation. CREDIT: identifies atrial fibrillation (7); recognizes it as the likely embolic source for mesenteric ischemia (4); no acute STEMI (2); systematic read (2). PENALTY: missing/ misidentifying the AF (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes it as the likely embolic source for mesenteric ischemia
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no discrete P waves) — the likely embolic source. No acute ST elevation.
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no discrete P waves) — the likely embolic source. No acute ST elevation.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no discrete P waves) — the likely embolic source. No acute ST elevation.

coreActions.item: recognizes it as the likely embolic source for mesenteric ischemia

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = nonspecific/normal; no free air early. CREDIT: identifies a normal/nonspecific film (5); explicitly looks for and excludes free air (perforation) (5). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
criticalFindings: Nonspecific; importantly NO free air early (free air would indicate perforation from infarcted bowel).
steps.idealAnswer: Nonspecific; importantly NO free air early (free air would indicate perforation from infarcted bowel).

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Nonspecific; importantly NO free air early (free air would indicate perforation from infarcted bowel).

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the elevated lactate and CTA, what is your diagnosis and reasoning?
steps.rubric: Acute mesenteric ischemia, likely embolic (SMA) (6). Links AF + stopped anticoagulation → embolus; lactate + acidosis indicate ischemia (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute mesenteric ischemia, likely embolic — the atrial fibrillation with self-discontinued apixaban is the embolic source, and CTA shows an SMA filling defect/occlusion with segmental bowel-wall changes. The elevated lactate (~4.5) and metabolic acidosis confirm evolving bowel ischemia.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH abdominal pain (vascular infarct, 'can't-miss'): aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (CANT-MISS,5); STAT surgery + vascular/IR consult for revascularization (CANT-MISS,6); broad-spectrum antibiotics (4); systemic anticoagulation/heparin (4); NPO + NG decompression (3); CT angiography (2). PENALTY: discharging 'pain out of proportion' (-4); splanchnic vasoconstrictors worsening ischemia (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Surgical/vascular emergency: pain out of proportion to exam. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, NPO, NG decompression. Systemic anticoagulation (heparin). STAT surgery and vascular/IR consults for revascularization or resection. CT angiography. Avoid vasopressors that worsen splanchnic flow.

criticalActions.item: STAT surgery / vascular consult for revascularization

requiredActions: STAT surgery / vascular consult for revascularization

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 72F with atrial fibrillation presents with sudden, severe, diffuse abdominal pain that seems out of proportion to a relatively benign abdominal exam. The diagnosis is Acute mesenteric ischemia, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: soft, mild diffuse tenderness EARLY without rebound or rigidity (pain out of proportion to exam); bowel sounds reduced. (Peritoneal signs would develop late once infarction occurs.)
Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmur. Rectal: occult/frank blood if examined.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Acute mesenteric ischemia while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Acute mesenteric ischemia and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
managementPearls: STAT surgery / vascular consult for revascularization
